notes.note: The letter-heading. M'. Acilius Glabrio, proconsul of Sicily 46–45 BC, is assured of the special weight of this particular recommendation, which rests on Caesar's own confirmation.

notes.anchor: and so he is now called Publius Cornelius
notes.note: Upon receiving Roman citizenship, a new citizen adopted the praenomen and nomen of his patron; P. Cornelius Dolabella (cos. suff. 44 BC) obtained the grant for Demetrius Megas, who accordingly became "Publius Cornelius" while retaining his Greek cognomen Megas ("great") as an unofficial identifier.

notes.anchor: the tablet on which the names of those granted citizenship were inscribed to be torn down
notes.note: After rumours spread that officials were selling Caesar's grants, Caesar ordered the tabula (bronze tablet) listing enfranchised individuals to be taken down (revellere). This was a drastic act because such tablets were the legal proof of status; Cicero stresses that Caesar verbally guaranteed Megas's grant was unaffected, providing an oral confirmation in lieu of the destroyed documentary evidence.

notes.anchor: I commend him to you with such zeal that I could commend no one with greater
notes.note: Cicero's letters of recommendation (litterae commendaticiae) operated on a sliding scale of warmth. This superlative formula — "I could commend no one more strongly" — is his highest grade, deployed rarely and designed to signal to the governor that the sender's personal honour is at stake in the treatment of the recommendee.
